name: German Cockroach
tagline: Most common household pest in the Philippines
description: The German cockroach is the most prevalent cockroach species found in Philippine homes, restaurants, and food establishments. They thrive in warm, humid environments and reproduce rapidly, making infestations difficult to control without professional help.
signs: Droppings resembling black pepper near food sources
Egg cases (oothecae) in hidden corners or under appliances
Musty odor in heavily infested areas
Live or dead cockroaches near drains and under sinks
prevention: Seal all food in airtight containers
Fix leaking pipes and eliminate standing water
Seal cracks and crevices in walls and cabinets
Take out garbage daily and keep bins covered
treatment: Gel baits placed in cracks and crevices are highly effective. Combined with boric acid dust in voids and IGR (Insect Growth Regulator) sprays, most infestations can be resolved in 2–4 weeks.
dangerLevel: Carries bacteria like Salmonella and E. coli; triggers asthma and allergies

name: Aedes Mosquito
tagline: Primary vector of dengue, Zika, and chikungunya
description: Aedes aegypti is the primary mosquito species responsible for transmitting dengue fever across the Philippines. It is a daytime biter, most active during early morning and late afternoon, and breeds in clean, stagnant water.
signs: Itchy bites appearing during daytime
Mosquito larvae visible in stagnant water containers
High-pitched buzzing near ears
Presence near flower pots, water drums, and tires
prevention: Eliminate standing water in containers every week (4S campaign)
Use mosquito nets and window screens
Apply EPA-approved insect repellent when outdoors
Wear long sleeves during peak biting hours
treatment: Fogging and larviciding for large-scale infestations. For homes, use aerosol sprays labeled for mosquitoes and install screen doors and windows. Seek a licensed pest control provider for community-level treatment.
dangerLevel: Transmits dengue, chikungunya, Zika virus — can be fatal if untreated

name: Subterranean Termite
description: Subterranean termites are the most destructive pest in the Philippines, responsible for billions of pesos in structural damage annually. They live in underground colonies and travel through mud tubes to reach wood, often causing severe damage before detection.
signs: Mud tubes on walls, floors, or foundations
Hollow-sounding wood when tapped
Discarded wings near windows after swarming
Bubbling or uneven paint on wooden surfaces
prevention: Avoid wood-to-soil contact in construction
Apply pre-construction soil treatment with termiticide
Keep firewood and lumber away from the house
Ensure proper drainage around the foundation
treatment: Soil treatment with liquid termiticide creates a chemical barrier. Baiting systems are effective for ongoing colony elimination. Severe infestations require professional treatment — DIY methods are rarely sufficient.
dangerLevel: Can compromise structural integrity of buildings; very costly to repair

name: Norway Rat
tagline: Major carrier of leptospirosis and other diseases
description: The Norway rat (Rattus norvegicus) and the roof rat (Rattus rattus) are the two most common rat species in Philippine urban areas. They contaminate food, damage property, and pose serious health risks through their urine and droppings.
signs: Droppings along walls and near food sources
Gnaw marks on food packaging, wires, or wood
Burrows along walls or in garden soil
Greasy rub marks along frequently traveled paths
prevention: Store food in sealed metal or glass containers
Block entry points larger than 1 cm with steel wool or metal plates
Keep surroundings clean and clutter-free
Never leave pet food out overnight
treatment: Snap traps and rodenticide bait stations placed along known runways. For large infestations, engage a licensed pest control professional. Always use tamper-resistant bait stations where children and pets are present.
dangerLevel: Spreads leptospirosis, rat-bite fever, hantavirus, and salmonellosis
season: Year-round; peaks during and after flooding

name: Common Bed Bug
tagline: Notorious nighttime biter hiding in mattresses
description: Bed bugs are small, flat, parasitic insects that feed exclusively on blood. They are most active at night and hide in mattress seams, bed frames, and furniture cracks during the day. Their resurgence is linked to increased travel.
signs: Small, itchy red welts in a line or cluster on skin
Tiny dark or rust-colored spots on mattress seams
Sweet, musty odor in heavily infested rooms
Shed exoskeletons and egg casings in crevices
prevention: Inspect secondhand furniture before bringing it home
Encase mattresses in bed bug-proof covers
Reduce clutter where bed bugs can hide
Check hotel rooms thoroughly when traveling
treatment: Heat treatment (above 50°C) is the most effective method. Combined with residual insecticide application in cracks and crevices. Multiple treatments 2 weeks apart are usually required.
dangerLevel: Not known to transmit disease but causes significant sleep disruption and anxiety

name: Common House Fly
tagline: Contaminates food and spreads over 100 pathogens
description: The house fly breeds in decaying organic matter and animal waste, making it a major health hazard in Philippine markets and food areas. It regurgitates digestive fluid and defecates while feeding, contaminating food and surfaces.
signs: Cluster of flies near garbage, food waste, or animal areas
Fly specks (small dark dots) on walls and surfaces
Maggots in decomposing waste or poorly sealed trash
Persistent buzzing sounds around food preparation areas
prevention: Seal all garbage in covered bins and dispose of daily
Cover all food and use screens on windows and doors
Clean up pet waste promptly
Eliminate standing water and wet organic waste
treatment: Fly baits, sticky traps, and UV light traps for indoor control. Residual sprays on resting surfaces. Address the breeding source first — treatment without eliminating breeding sites will fail.
dangerLevel: Transmits cholera, typhoid, dysentery, and food poisoning bacteria
season: Year-round; peaks in summer and after flooding

name: Rice Weevil
tagline: Destroys stored rice and grain supplies
description: The rice weevil (Sitophilus oryzae) is one of the most destructive pests of stored grain in the Philippines. Adults bore into rice kernels to lay eggs, and larvae develop inside the grain, rendering entire sacks of rice unfit for consumption.
signs: Fine flour-like dust or powder inside rice sacks
Small holes in individual rice grains
Adult weevils (small, dark, snout-nosed beetles) in grain
Clumped or discolored rice with off smell
prevention: Store rice in airtight containers with bay leaves or neem leaves
Inspect newly purchased sacks before storage
Keep storage areas cool, dry, and well-ventilated
Freeze infested grain for 72 hours to kill all life stages
treatment: Remove and dispose of heavily infested grain. Clean storage bins thoroughly with soap and water. Apply food-grade diatomaceous earth to storage bins before refilling. For commercial storage, consult a pest control professional for fumigation.
dangerLevel: Economic pest — destroys food supply rather than posing direct health risk
season: Year-round; accelerates in warm, humid conditions

name: Green Peach Aphid
tagline: Sap-sucking pest of vegetables and ornamentals
description: Aphids are tiny, soft-bodied insects that cluster on the undersides of leaves and young shoots, sucking plant sap. They excrete honeydew, promoting fungal growth and sooty mold. They also transmit plant viruses.
signs: Clusters of small green, yellow, or black insects on new growth
Curled, yellowing, or distorted leaves
Sticky honeydew coating on leaves and surfaces below plant
Black sooty mold growing on honeydew deposits
prevention: Encourage natural predators like ladybugs and lacewings
Inspect new plants before introducing to the garden
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilizer which promotes soft growth aphids prefer
Use reflective mulch to deter winged aphids
treatment: Strong spray of water dislodges colonies. Neem oil or insecticidal soap are effective organic options. For severe infestations, use registered aphicide. Always spray the undersides of leaves.
dangerLevel: Causes plant yield loss and spreads plant viruses
season: Cool, dry months (November–February); may occur year-round

name: Mole Cricket
tagline: Underground pest that destroys roots and seedlings
description: Mole crickets are burrowing insects that damage rice paddies, vegetable farms, and lawns by tunneling through soil and severing plant roots. They are considered a delicacy (kamaro) in Pampanga but are serious agricultural pests elsewhere.
signs: Raised tunnels visible in soil surface after rain
Wilting or dying seedlings with severed roots
Spongy, soft soil in formerly firm lawn or field areas
Chirping sounds coming from soil at night
prevention: Flood fields before planting to drive out mole crickets
Encourage natural predators such as birds and ground beetles
Use resistant crop varieties where available
Avoid over-irrigation which creates favorable burrowing conditions
treatment: Bait applications of chlorpyrifos or carbaryl-based granules in early evening when mole crickets surface. Biological control using entomopathogenic nematodes (Steinernema spp.) is effective and environmentally safe.
dangerLevel: Causes significant seedling and turf loss; economic impact on rice farms

name: Brown Planthopper
tagline: Number one rice pest causing hopperburn
description: The brown planthopper (Nilaparvata lugens) is the most devastating rice pest in Asia, causing hopperburn — a condition where entire rice fields turn brown and die rapidly. It feeds on rice phloem and transmits grassy stunt and ragged stunt viruses.
signs: Circular patches of brown, dead rice plants (hopperburn)
Honeydew deposits and sooty mold at the base of plants
Dense colonies of small brown insects at plant base
Stunted plant growth and reduced tillering
prevention: Plant resistant rice varieties (e.g., IR64, NSIC Rc222)
Avoid excessive nitrogen application
Maintain proper water management in paddies
Preserve natural enemies by minimizing broad-spectrum pesticide use
treatment: Apply registered insecticides (buprofezin, dinotefuran) at economic threshold of 1 egg mass per hill or 2 adults per hill. Use alternate-wetting-and-drying irrigation. Consult your local DA/PhilRice extension officer for variety recommendations.
dangerLevel: Can destroy entire rice crop within 1–2 weeks if untreated
season: Peaks during wet season rice cropping (July–October)
